allergens: shellfish (tom yum paste may contain shrimp paste)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tom Yum Fried Rice with Egg (no meat) is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at a Burmese-Thai restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with egg and tom yum paste component estimates from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Tom Yum Fried Rice with Egg (no meat) exists in any primary approved source. Values are estimated by component analysis: cooked jasmine rice (USDA SR Legacy #20444, ~65% by weight), whole egg stir-fried (USDA SR Legacy #01123, ~15% by weight), tom yum paste and aromatics (FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues, ~10% by weight), cooking oil (USDA SR Legacy #04582, ~5% by weight), and vegetables/spring onion (~5% by weight). Sodium elevated due to fish sauce and tom yum paste. All values are per 100 g of the composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; Fried rice (jasmine rice base) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum Fried Rice with Egg. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for jasmine rice fried rice (GI ~64, medium), which forms approximately 65% of the dish by weight. The egg and tom yum paste components contribute negligible additional glycemic load. GL per serving = (64 × 25.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 57.1 g per 350 g serving. gi_category set to 'medium' per validator threshold (56–69 inclusive).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving) from fish sauce and tom yum paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (~1470 mg per 350 g serving), which represents approximately 64% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce and no added salt is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving (~57 g GL) from jasmine rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in small portions due to its medium GI (64) and high glycemic load per standard serving (~57 g), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting brown rice or reducing portion size to 150–200 g is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from egg and cooking oil, combined with high sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its combined sodium load (~1470 mg per serving) and cholesterol contribution from egg (~217 mg per serving); choosing a smaller portion and requesting less oil and sauce reduces cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Fried Rice with Egg (No Meat) is a Southeast Asian-influenced fried rice dish popular in Burmese-Thai fusion restaurants in Singapore, including at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. The dish is prepared by stir-frying cooked jasmine rice in a wok with tom yum paste — a fragrant Thai spice blend containing lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chilli, and shrimp paste — along with beaten egg, fish sauce, and cooking oil. The result is a boldly flavoured, aromatic rice dish with a spicy, sour, and umami profile characteristic of tom yum seasoning. The egg provides a soft, slightly custardy texture contrast to the individual grains of rice. As a meat-free variant, it relies on egg and the umami depth of fish sauce and tom yum paste for protein and flavour. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal per 100 g) with a medium glycemic index (~64) and a high glycemic load per standard serving due to the jasmine rice base. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) from fish sauce and tom yum paste, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains egg, fish, and potential shellfish allergens from the paste. It is commonly served as a one-dish meal for lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Tom Yum Fried Rice with Egg (no meat) was found in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using jasmine fried rice as the closest analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated (not directly measured) from USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA sources; a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish would improve confidence to high. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for estimation. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for estimation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero by formulation — no hydrogenated fats are used in this dish; cooking oil and egg are the only fat sources). 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice fried rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is 'medium' per validator threshold (56–69 inclusive). 6. The food_name in the input JSON ('Tom Yum rice ( တုံယန်းထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်, no meat)') contains Burmese script indicating 'Tom Yum rice, fried egg included, no meat'; the canonical English name has been normalised to 'Tom Yum Fried Rice with Egg (No Meat)'. 7. Cuisine tags in the input ('Noodle', 'Lor Mee') appear to be misclassified and have been disregarded; this is a rice dish, not a noodle dish. 8. Halal status is marked false because tom yum paste commonly contains shrimp paste and fish sauce; halal-certified variants may exist but cannot be confirmed for this specific restaurant. 9. The dish is served at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant, 77 High Street, Singapore, sourced from field survey data at SGD 7.00.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built. Identified that cuisine is Burmese (served in Singapore context) requiring Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi multilingual names in addition to Thai script for the dish's Thai culinary origin.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Confirmed all strings match verbatim. Verified GI category threshold (GI 64 = medium, 56–69 range). Confirmed -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are documented in notes. Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 is documented. Confirmed ontology match_type values are within allowed set (exact, closest_parent, none). Final self-score: 8/8, all hard gates pass. Decision: PASS.
